text: The surgeon replaces four ports, each measuring 8.5 millimeters, and enters the peritoneal cavity targeting the left lower quadrant. Adhesions are taken down, and the small intestine is retracted from the pelvis using four by fours and the fourth arm. Sharp dissection is performed next to the sigmoid colon and towards the pelvis, with the left ureter visible.

The surgeon mobilizes the distal part of the sigmoid colon, creating a mesenteric window with a bipolar energy device to divide the blood supply. The left ureter is visible as the dissection progresses into the pelvis. The sigmoid colon is completely immobilized distally, preparing for transection and anastomosis. The articulation aids in dissection, and a sponge is used for gentle retraction. The colon is visibly filled with blood, and further division of the mesentery is performed while mobilizing the descending colon.

The surgeon moves cephalad to prepare the sigmoid colon for transection. Indocyanine green (ICG) is injected intravenously, highlighting the blood supply and marking the area for transection. A bipolar energy device is used to divide the mesentery and the bowel instead of staplers.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis on the unprepped bowel, using two size 3-0 absorbable barbed sutures placed in the back wall for retraction and exposure. The back wall is sutured together in a running fashion until reaching both corners, followed by a canal suture technique from outside in and inside out.

The surgeon continues the anastomosis using an outside-in, inside-out suturing technique, progressively tightening each suture until reaching the final portion. The sutures are left loose to expose the lumen and ensure proper layer alignment. The robotic fourth arm assists in exposure but is then moved aside. ICG is injected intravenously to assess the blood supply at both ends of the anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the suturing of the back wall of the anastomosis, ensuring proper division of attachments. The pelvis is filled with water to check for anastomotic leaks, with no bubbles observed, indicating a negative test. ICG is used again to evaluate the blood supply, confirming proper perfusion.
